Article Title: Quote of the Day by Accenture CEO Julie Sweet: Have the confidence to focus on uncharted territory
Article Text:
Quote of the Day by Accenture CEO Julie Sweet

Quote of the day by Accenture CEO Julie Sweet



“Have the confidence to focus on uncharted territory. Take chances.”

What Julie Sweet's quote means



A message reflected in her work



The takeaway



Julie Sweet has built her reputation as the CEO of Accenture, one of the world's largest professional services companies with over 700,000 employees across more than 120 countries, on a leadership style that favours direct, actionable thinking over vague inspiration. This quote fits squarely within that pattern, and is essentially an instruction that says confidence comes first, followed by the uncharted territory. Chances follow only once the first two are in place.The quote works almost like a sequence rather than a single idea. Sweet does not say people should simply “take chances,” which is the version of this advice most commonly repeated and most easily ignored. She places confidence at the very start of the sentence, essentially as the precondition for everything that follows. Without it, uncharted territory remains just unexplored, avoided and left for someone else. Confidence, in Sweet's framing, enables a condition that has to exist before someone is willing to step toward the unfamiliar in the first place.The word “focus” is doing quieter but equally important work here.Sweet is describing deliberate attention directed at the parts of a business, a market or a career that remain unmapped. She says that the uncharted territory is a space that requires focus precisely because it lacks the existing frameworks, playbooks, or precedents that guide decision-making everywhere else.Only once confidence and focus are established does Sweet arrive at the final instruction: take chances. As per the executive, chances taken without confidence tend to be reactive, defensive or half-committed. Chances taken without focus tend to be scattered and difficult to sustain. Sweet's quote suggests that the quality of a chance is inseparable from the mental posture behind it.Sweet has repeatedly pointed to Accenture's own evolution as evidence of this principle in practice. The company has moved through multiple eras of technological disruption, which includes cloud computing, digital transformation, and now generative AI, and in each case, the businesses and leaders willing to move into unfamiliar territory ahead of certainty were the ones who ended up shaping the market rather than reacting to it.She has spoken publicly about the current AI moment in particular, describing it as a period where the biggest risk is not moving too fast into the unknown, but staying too long in familiar, well-understood territory while competitors experiment their way toward an advantage. Under her leadership, Accenture has pushed investment into emerging and unproven areas of AI application, often before client demand or industry consensus has fully caught up.That is a direct expression of the quote's logic: confidence precedes the willingness to explore, and the willingness to explore precedes the chances that eventually define competitive advantage.The quote applies differently depending on where someone sits. For someone early in their career, it is a reminder that caution can quietly close off the very opportunities that would otherwise define a career trajectory. For someone mid-career, it is a useful prompt to examine whether recent choices have genuinely stretched into new territory, or simply repeated safer, already-proven moves.
Article Summary: Julie Sweet is the CEO of Accenture, one of the world's largest professional services companies. Her quote is essentially an instruction that says confidence comes first, followed by the uncharted territory. Chances follow only once the first two are in place.

Article Title: After raising prices, Apple will now let you 'rent' iPhones, iPads and Macs
Article Text:
FILE - The iPhone 17 Pro is displayed during an announcement of new products at Apple Park on Tuesday, Sept. 9, 2025, in Cupertino, Calif. (AP Photo/Godofredo A. Vásquez, File)

How Apple Upgrade works, and which devices don't qualify

Apple is preparing to launch Apple Upgrade, a lease-to-own program for most of its hardware lineup, on July 28 in the US. The service will cover most iPhone, Mac, iPad and Apple Watch models, and it works less like a payment plan and more like a car lease—you pay monthly, and at the end of the term you keep the device, hand it back, or roll into something newer. Bloomberg's Mark Gurman first reported the plan, with Klarna signing on as the financial backer.It doesn't take much to work out why now. Apple raised prices by hundreds of dollars across Macs, iPads and other devices last month, blaming an industry-wide memory shortage that the AI data-centre boom has made considerably worse. The iPhone escaped that round, but nobody expects it to escape September. Apple Upgrade lands right in that gap, and Apple reportedly plans to pitch it as the cheaper monthly option versus its existing financing.Lease terms run 24 months for iPhones and Apple Watches, and 36 months for Macs and iPads. Signing up requires a soft credit check. Paying off early, upgrading mid-term or keeping the device at the end are all on the table, though in some cases those moves come with an extra fee. AsThere are gaps. AppleCare is not bundled in, unlike the current iPhone Upgrade Program—a notable omission given AppleCare pricing has also crept up.Cheaper hardware is excluded too: the Apple Watch SE, the entry-level iPad, the iPhone 16 and the fast-selling MacBook Neo are all ineligible. Business and education purchases are out as well.Apple will stop taking new sign-ups for the iPhone Upgrade Program and standard financing to clear the runway. It's worth remembering Apple built and then killed its own in-house hardware subscription in 2024. Partnering with Klarna gets Apple the same customer lock-in without carrying the credit risk.
Article Summary: Apple will launch Apple Upgrade on July 28 in the US. Service will cover most iPhone, Mac, iPad and Apple Watch models. You pay monthly, and at the end of the term you keep the device, hand it back, or roll into something newer.

Article Title: Google launches ‘more efficient’ Gemini 3.6 Flash AI model along with 3.5 Flash-Lite AI
Article Text:
Gemini 3.6 Flash brings better performance at lower cost

Gemini 3.5 Flash-Lite targets faster AI workloads

Google has unveiled Gemini 3.6 Flash and Gemini 3.5 Flash-Lite AI models, aimed at developers building AI agents and enterprise applications. The company said the new models offer better speed, lower costs and improved performance than earlier Flash models. Gemini 3.6 Flash is designed for coding, knowledge work and multimodal tasks, while Gemini 3.5 Flash-Lite focuses on high-volume workloads that require low latency. Google also said it has started training its next-generation Gemini 4 model and plans to make Gemini 3.5 Pro widely available after testing with partners.Google said Gemini 3.6 Flash builds on feedback received from developers using Gemini 3.5 Flash. According to the company, the new model uses 17% fewer output tokens than Gemini 3.5 Flash, based on the Artificial Analysis Index. In some coding benchmarks, including DeepSWE by Datacurve, output token usage dropped by as much as 65%.Google said the model also delivers better coding, document analysis, report drafting and multimodal capabilities while reducing the number of reasoning steps and tool calls needed to complete complex tasks.The company has priced Gemini 3.6 Flash at $1.50 per one million input tokens and $7.50 per one million output tokens.Alongside the new flagship Flash model, Google also introduced Gemini 3.5 Flash-Lite, which it describes as the fastest model in the Gemini 3.5 family. According to the Artificial Analysis Index, the model can generate 350 output tokens per second. Google said it is designed for high-volume tasks such as AI search, document processing and other agent-based workflows.The company has priced Flash-Lite at $0.30 per one million input tokens and $2.50 per one million output tokens.Google said the model also includes built-in computer-use capabilities and outperforms previous Flash-Lite models in coding and real-world task execution.
Article Summary: Gemini 3.6 Flash is designed for coding, knowledge work and multimodal tasks. Gemini 3.5 Flash-Lite focuses on high-volume workloads that require low latency. Google also said it has started training its next-generation Gemini 4 model.

Article Title: Meta's 'highest-paid' employee Alexandr Wang has forgotten who the world's biggest internet company is, says: Who …
Article Text:

3.6 Flash delivers higher precision with fewer unwanted code edits and reduced execution loops, as seen in DeepSWE (49% vs. 37%), and shows significant improvement in ML Research, as seen in MLE Bench (63.9% vs. 49.7%).

It has improved computer use capabilities as seen in OSWorld-Verified (83.0% vs. 78.4%). Computer use is now a built-in client side tool via the Gemini API and Gemini Enterprise.

It outperforms 3.5 Flash in knowledge work, as shown by benchmarks like GDPval-AA v2 (1421 vs. 1349). Customers like Hebbia and Harvey have found it particularly capable at multimodal tasks like document parsing, chart and data analysis, and report drafting.

Meta’s highest paid employee Alexandr Wang recently shared a post on X (formerly Twitter) taunting Google’s Gemini model. Wang was responding to a leaderboard shared by an X user – Tae Kim depicting Meta's Muse Spark 1.1 outperforming the world's biggest internet company’s latest AI model – Gemini 3.6 Flash.the post stated. The chart quoted in the post from Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index showed top models like Anthropic’s Claude Fable 5 and OpenAI’s GPT-5.6 (59) leading, with Gemini 3.6 Flash scoring 50 while Meta's entry ranks higher in reasoning, coding, and agentic tasks.Alexandr Wang shared the post on his timeline, writingunderscoring Meta's rapid AI progress since his high-profile 2025 hire from Scale AI. For those unaware, Mark Zuckerberg hired Alexandr Wang as Meta's Chief AI Officer after a reported $15 billion deal.Muse Spark is the company’s first model developed by Meta Superintelligence Labs which is led by Alexandr Wang. Earlier this month, Meta launched Muse Spark 1.1 as a new version of Muse Spark – a multimodal AI model designed for agentic coding. Announcing the model then, the company said,Following the launch, AI researcher François Chollet called Meta's Muse Spark aandWang welcomed the feedback and responded to the criticism, saying,Alexandr Wang’s comment on Gemini model comes on the same day when Google launched Gemini 3.6 Flash, claimed to beGoogle said in its official announcement.Stating how Gemini 3.6 Flash is better than the previous model – Gemini 3.5 Flash, the company said:
Article Summary: Meta's highest paid employee Alexandr Wang recently shared a post on X (formerly Twitter) taunting Google’s Gemini model. Wang was responding to a leaderboard shared by an X user – Tae Kim depicting Meta's Muse Spark 1.1 outperforming Google's latest AI model – Gemini 3.6 Flash. The chart quoted in the post from Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index showed top models like Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 and OpenAI's GPT-5.
